briefing.headline: 6 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: AJEM, EMJ, ALiEM. Main themes: reviews, trials, guideline or formal recommendation. Best early reads: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management; Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches.
briefing.editorialHeadline: CAP de-escalation, hyperkalaemia basics, and pragmatic updates across the ED
briefing.editorialSummary: Today's set is strongest where it tightens common ED decisions rather than overturning them. The clearest practice signals are the 2025 CAP guideline update pushing shorter treatment and more selective antibiotics, the hyperkalaemia review reinforcing calcium-first management for unstable patients while moving older binders further out of the acute toolbox, and the pediatric sepsis fluid trial showing no clinically meaningful advantage for balanced fluid over saline. Around that core, several papers sharpen risk stratification and workflow: toxicology with a toxidrome-first framework, sedation safety, stroke selection, geriatric medication harm, and the limits of prehospital or biomarker shortcuts. Overall theme: be faster on the fundamentals, and more disciplined about where evidence does—or does not—justify escalation.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Revisit your default CAP pathway: use imaging deliberately, avoid automatic antibiotics when the clinical picture is convincingly viral, and think in terms of minimum-effective duration rather than fixed longer courses.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The 2025 ATS update on CAP matters because it nudges ED care toward narrower, shorter treatment rather than reflexive broad coverage.
dailyEditorial.summary: The 2025 ATS update on CAP matters because it nudges ED care toward narrower, shorter treatment rather than reflexive broad coverage. The practical signals are stronger support for expert-performed lung ultrasound, more willingness to withhold antibiotics when a convincing viral diagnosis is present in otherwise low-risk patients, and acceptance of shorter courses for stable non-severe disease.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For dangerous hyperkalaemia, do the basics well: calcium for membrane stabilization, insulin-glucose plus salbutamol for shift, close glucose monitoring afterward, and early dialysis planning when the physiology is not going to cooperate.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review is a useful reset on acute hyperkalaemia: stabilize first, shift potassium second, and be honest about what actually works in the ED.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review is a useful reset on acute hyperkalaemia: stabilize first, shift potassium second, and be honest about what actually works in the ED. Calcium remains the immediate priority when ECG change or marked hyperkalaemia is present, insulin-glucose still does most of the intracellular shifting, and inhaled beta-agonists are worth combining early. Older resin-based strategies continue to lose ground.

dailyEditorial.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: In pediatric septic shock, either saline or balanced crystalloid is reasonable; focus your energy on early recognition, reassessment after each bolus, and rapid escalation when shock persists.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PRoMPT BOLUS is valuable less for proving a winner than for removing a distraction.
dailyEditorial.summary: PRoMPT BOLUS is valuable less for proving a winner than for removing a distraction. In children treated for septic shock, balanced crystalloid did not outperform 0.9% saline on major outcomes, which supports pragmatic local practice and keeps attention on timing, source control, vasoactive support, and overall resuscitation quality rather than fluid tribalism.

dailyEditorial.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For suspected LVO, let local transport times and thrombectomy access drive routing decisions; whichever pathway you use, protect imaging-to-reperfusion time as the metric that matters.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This meta-analysis addresses a systems question every stroke network wrestles with: bypass to thrombectomy capability versus initial stabilization and transfer.
dailyEditorial.summary: This meta-analysis addresses a systems question every stroke network wrestles with: bypass to thrombectomy capability versus initial stabilization and transfer. The literature remains heterogeneous, but the paper is still useful because it frames the tradeoff explicitly around time to reperfusion rather than institutional habit.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When the toxin is unclear, build the case from syndrome, timeline, and expected trajectory, then get TOXBASE or poisons input early rather than waiting for diagnostic certainty.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The RCEM/NPIS guideline is strong because it resists pretending unidentified poisoning can be reduced to one algorithm.
dailyEditorial.summary: The RCEM/NPIS guideline is strong because it resists pretending unidentified poisoning can be reduced to one algorithm. Its value is in structuring first-pass ED care around toxidromes, toxicokinetics, and expected clinical evolution, while making clear that generic guidance should quickly hand off to agent-specific advice when available.

dailyEditorial.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: In older patients, actively look for medication-caused or medication-worsened illness and use the ED visit to stop, simplify, or flag high-risk drugs where appropriate.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This narrative review argues that medication harm in older adults is not a side issue in emergency care—it is part of the presenting illness burden.
dailyEditorial.summary: This narrative review argues that medication harm in older adults is not a side issue in emergency care—it is part of the presenting illness burden. The practical contribution is a reminder that reconciliation, deprescribing, and pharmacist-supported review can be active ED interventions, not just ward tidy-up tasks.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Ketamine remains a solid pediatric sedation option, but use this review as a prompt to tighten preparation, airway vigilance, and parent counseling rather than to become complacent because the drug is familiar.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: A large safety synthesis on pediatric IV ketamine is useful because ketamine remains common, familiar, and occasionally under-scrutinized.
dailyEditorial.summary: A large safety synthesis on pediatric IV ketamine is useful because ketamine remains common, familiar, and occasionally under-scrutinized. Pooling adverse event rates and reported predictors helps calibrate risk more realistically than single-center anecdotes, even if prediction remains imperfect.

dailyEditorial.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your service has a refractory VF protocol, vector change or DSED deserves serious consideration—but only within a rehearsed system that preserves compressions, charging workflow, and safety.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This DOSE-VF secondary analysis supports the idea that refractory VF care may benefit from changing shock strategy rather than simply repeating standard defibrillation.
dailyEditorial.summary: This DOSE-VF secondary analysis supports the idea that refractory VF care may benefit from changing shock strategy rather than simply repeating standard defibrillation. The mechanistic signal is shorter time spent in VF, especially with DSED, which is more clinically interesting than a purely theoretical impedance discussion.

dailyEditorial.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not let “minor” be the whole decision, but do require a clearly measurable disabling deficit before reaching for IV thrombolysis in borderline cases.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The main contribution here is restraint.
dailyEditorial.summary: The main contribution here is restraint. In minor stroke, the paper argues against treating every mild deficit as thrombolysis-worthy and instead pushes toward a more granular view of what counts as truly disabling.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use these data to examine where delay accumulates—scene, resuscitation, or transport—while avoiding simplistic blame based on total prehospital time alone.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PATOS adds another reminder that prehospital time metrics matter, but interpretation is messy because long times often track with sicker patients.
dailyEditorial.summary: PATOS adds another reminder that prehospital time metrics matter, but interpretation is messy because long times often track with sicker patients. The study is still useful for systems work because it separates components of delay rather than treating transport time as one undifferentiated number.

dailyEditorial.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If STI screening is inconsistent in your department, digital prompts or decision aids may be worth testing—but success will depend on workflow fit more than on the tool alone.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This trial is less about STI testing itself than about whether patient-facing decision support can reliably move ED preventive care.
dailyEditorial.summary: This trial is less about STI testing itself than about whether patient-facing decision support can reliably move ED preventive care. That makes it operationally interesting for departments trying to improve screening without relying entirely on clinician memory.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For MeVO stroke with meaningful deficits, do not downplay the case as “too distal”; involve stroke and endovascular teams early and keep door-to-decision time tight.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO signal is important because medium-vessel occlusion has been a gray zone between “definitely thrombectomy” and “best medical therapy.” A positive functional outcome signal is enough to keep this subgro…
dailyEditorial.summary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO signal is important because medium-vessel occlusion has been a gray zone between “definitely thrombectomy” and “best medical therapy.” A positive functional outcome signal is enough to keep this subgroup on the front foot during stroke triage, even if external validation still matters.

dailyEditorial.title: The prevalence of life-threatening diagnoses in emergency department patients with chest pain in a national group
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use prevalence data to steady your pre-test thinking, not to reassure yourself prematurely; ACS still dominates the dangerous end of the chest pain differential.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prevalence study is useful calibration.
dailyEditorial.summary: This prevalence study is useful calibration. Most atraumatic chest pain is not catastrophic, but the serious-diagnosis rate is high enough that a casual or overly pathway-driven assessment still risks missing important disease.

dailyEditorial.title: Major adverse cardiac events in patients with indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin testing and chest pain: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If the troponin is indeterminate, default to serial interpretation and whole-patient risk assessment rather than forcing a decision from a single borderline result.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Indeterminate hs-troponin results remain a workflow trap, and this review highlights how inconsistent current management still is.
dailyEditorial.summary: Indeterminate hs-troponin results remain a workflow trap, and this review highlights how inconsistent current management still is. The key message is not that indeterminate values are benign, but that isolated numbers without serial context do not finish the job.

dailyEditorial.title: Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation for Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For pediatric OHCA, follow local ECPR pathways if they exist, but do not over-interpret adult literature when counseling teams or families about expected benefit.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Pediatric ECPR for OHCA remains a highly consequential but under-evidenced area.
dailyEditorial.summary: Pediatric ECPR for OHCA remains a highly consequential but under-evidenced area. This paper is useful mainly because it makes the gap explicit: adult enthusiasm cannot simply be pasted onto children without stronger comparative data and mature local systems.

dailyEditorial.title: High-Risk Medication Prescribing Among Older Adults in the Emergency Department: A National Assessment
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Before discharging older adults, give new prescriptions the same scrutiny you give admission meds—especially sedatives, anticholinergics, and other familiar high-risk choices.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This national assessment complements the broader geriatric medication review by showing that discharge prescribing risk remains common even as overall rates improve.
dailyEditorial.summary: This national assessment complements the broader geriatric medication review by showing that discharge prescribing risk remains common even as overall rates improve. The important point is that progress has been partial and uneven, not solved.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between initial bandemia degree and short-term mortality among adults with bacteremia in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Marked bandemia in bacteremia should increase your vigilance and disposition caution, even if it does not independently dictate antibiotics or source-control decisions.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Bandemia is not a magic marker, but this study suggests it still carries prognostic information in bacteremic adults.
dailyEditorial.summary: Bandemia is not a magic marker, but this study suggests it still carries prognostic information in bacteremic adults. That matters less as a trigger for specific therapy and more as another signal against underestimating illness severity when the patient otherwise looks only moderately unwell.

dailyEditorial.title: Clinical Features, CT Imaging Decisions and Yield by Age in Adults With Abdominal Pain in the Emergency Department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: In older adults with abdominal pain, trust the benign-looking exam less and your imaging threshold less rigidly; age changes both presentation and test yield.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This age-stratified abdominal pain paper reinforces a familiar bedside truth: exam findings get less trustworthy as patients get older, while imaging yield rises.
dailyEditorial.summary: This age-stratified abdominal pain paper reinforces a familiar bedside truth: exam findings get less trustworthy as patients get older, while imaging yield rises. Its practical value is in legitimizing a lower threshold for CT in selected older adults rather than pretending one abdominal pain heuristic fits all ages.

dailyEditorial.title: Prehospital prediction of clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis: a prospective cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use prehospital sepsis scores to heighten attention and resource planning, not to substitute for repeated bedside reassessment once the patient hits the ED.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prehospital sepsis cohort is a good argument against score worship.
dailyEditorial.summary: This prehospital sepsis cohort is a good argument against score worship. qSOFA and other variables carry some signal, but no single tool reliably predicts severity, shock, and mortality together, which is exactly what frontline clinicians often wish they would do.

dailyEditorial.title: Civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury in South-East Norway
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For potentially salvageable penetrating TBI, the challenge is often protocol execution rather than protocol invention: image early, coordinate neurosurgery fast, and close obvious compliance gaps.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The Norway pTBI series is less about changing first principles than about exposing implementation gaps.
dailyEditorial.summary: The Norway pTBI series is less about changing first principles than about exposing implementation gaps. Survivors can do well, but only if teams actually deliver the aggressive protocolized care they claim to believe in—antibiotics, ICP strategy, vascular imaging, and timely operative review.
